title: Clank & Slop — The Tape
tagline: — READ THE TAPE · ALL THE SLOP THAT'S FIT TO PRICE —

props.label: At a Glance · From the Tape Desk

bullets.text: The Hormuz premium has left the barrel. Brent traded around $73.65 after touching $73.22, its weakest level since February 27, as the strait read as normalising and Iranian crude re-entered the market. Oman opened two temporary north-south corridors with grouped departures and no tolls, US Energy Secretary Chris Wright put the last 24 hours' exit at some 20 million barrels, and Reuters described physical crude markets as mired in discounts.

A strong rebound is still not restored routine. CENTCOM counted 55 merchant ships carrying more than 17 million barrels in a single day — roughly 40% of the 125-to-138-per-day pre-war norm cited by Reuters, Clarksons and JMIC. Iran's gatekeeper leverage has gone dormant rather than disappeared: Oman and Iran are still discussing future administration and costs through a joint working group, and the residual risk is the nuclear-inspection fight, not the lane itself.

Compute is becoming territory, not a cloud line item. The binding constraint is moving toward electrons and memory: the DOE issued $17.5bn in conditional nuclear loans targeting 10 large reactors, a single hyperscale build (Project Kilby) is sized at ~2.67 GW at full build-out with first power targeted for 2028, and capacity is now sited, powered, financed and politically prioritized.

The money is moving even where the capability cannot yet be priced. Qualcomm bought Modular for ~$4bn to buy software that lets compute move across silicon, SK Hynix is raising up to $29.4bn for AI memory, and the dollar hit a 13-month high on rate-hike bets and a stock rout — capital chasing the buildout while no independent eval of the latest frontier claims has appeared.

props.title: Hormuz: a strong print, not a routine
props.subtitle: Strait transit reality · vessels per day
props.source: Reuters/Clarksons/JMIC (~125–138/day pre-war commercial norm), CENTCOM (55 merchant ships)

items.what: Hormuz: post-window fee risk and insured tonnage
items.why: The lane is open with no tolls during the temporary Omani corridors, but Iran's leverage is dormant, not gone — Oman and Iran are still negotiating future administration and costs through a joint working group, so the durable question is whether passage becomes an administered privilege once the window closes rather than whether the gate reopens.

items.what: IAEA verification · access to the bombed sites
items.why: Sanctions relief is moving through OFAC's license to 21 August while Grossi insists inspections will go ahead under the 60-day MOU clock and Iran bars current access to Fordow, Natanz and Isfahan except under a final deal — relief is arriving in the part Washington controls before enforceable limits exist in the part Tehran can delay.

items.what: Compute-as-territory buildout · power, memory and software
items.why: The binding constraint is shifting from chips to electrons and memory capacity: DOE conditional nuclear loans, a ~2.67 GW hyperscale site targeting 2028 first power, Qualcomm's ~$4bn Modular purchase and SK Hynix's up-to-$29.4bn memory raise are all sited, financed and politically prioritized infrastructure rather than cloud capacity.

The Security Council has demanded the RSF halt an imminent offensive on a city the HRC says puts ~500,000 civilians at risk; about ten consecutive days of drone strikes and encirclement are running without verified ground entry, so the prevention window is open precisely because the full assault is not yet confirmed.

items.what: Sovereign cyber-AI race · frontier claims vs verification
items.why: China's 360 has claimed a domestic counterpart to Anthropic's Mythos for vulnerability discovery and automated cyber defence, the capability US export controls sought to limit; no independent evaluation of Sakana's Fugu Ultra has appeared and Anthropic has not named the Qwable distill, so the open question is whether controls accelerate national substitution faster than anyone can verify.

items.what: Reuters described global physical crude as mired in discounts as stranded cargoes were released and Middle East supply ramped — cash Dubai, Oman and Murban trading at discounts, with ADNOC selling at least 48 million barrels of spot crude for June-to-August loading.

The DOE's $17.5bn in conditional nuclear loans targeting 10 large reactors reframes data-centre capacity as a generation problem; a single hyperscale site sized near 2.67 GW with first power targeted for 2028 shows the electron, not the chip, becoming the gating line.

SK Hynix is raising up to $29.4bn for AI memory and Qualcomm bought Modular for about $4bn for software that lets compute move across silicon — the bottleneck migrating off raw GPU count toward memory capacity and the layer that schedules it.
